emergent_themes.description: Having worked as a delivery rider creates unique empathy and economic awareness that influences ordering behavior and platform perception
Tension between health-conscious cooking aspirations and time pressure from academic demands, creating stress around meal preparation
Willingness to switch platforms for any amount of savings, even £1-2, driven by student budget constraints
Strategic email checking and voucher saving behavior that drives platform trial and switching

coding_summary: P037 demonstrates extreme price sensitivity and strategic platform switching behavior, driven by student budget constraints. Her unique experience as a delivery rider during COVID provides insider knowledge of delivery economics, making her more cost-conscious about fees. She exhibits a tension between health-conscious cooking aspirations (spending 1.5 hours making nutritionally balanced meals) and time pressure from academic demands. Her delivery behavior is highly strategic - primarily ordering groceries weekly through group orders or individually, with restaurant delivery reserved for weekends or when vouchers are available. She actively hunts for discounts through email checking and will switch platforms for any amount of savings. Social media influences her discovery of delivery options, and she values transparent student-specific discounts and permanent partner offers over temporary promotions.

_citations.cited_text: spk_1: I feel like the most stressful meals are definitely dinner. It is very time-consuming to make dinner because, with lunch, it can be something a bit more simple, like... just like a Greek yogurt bowl or something like that. But for dinner, I'm trying to get, like, everything in. So, for example, last night I think I spent, like, an hour and... like almost an hour and a half making dinner. And that's, like, stressful because I'm like, I could be getting work done right, right now! And I was like, this is pushing back my sleep as well. So that's definitely the most stressful meal of the week.

spk_1: So, I generally cook everything from scratch, or at least I try to be a little bit more health-conscious. So, I'll usually try and get, you know, every single component: like I get my protein, my carbs, and my vegetables. And if it's not vegetables in that meal, then I'll have fruit in place of that to eat after. But yeah, everything that I try and make is like... I make it all myself rather than having freezer food or pre-packaged food. I think it's just like... it makes me feel better about the food choices that I'm making.
spk_1: I think, in that case, I would definitely go for Deliveroo... even if it means, like, a small amount is off it. I mean, something is better than nothing.
spk_1: I feel like... any amount. Any amount of money... yeah.

spk_1: I think, in terms of food, it doesn't matter to me that much. I feel like I... as a uni student, you can't be very picky with, you know, kind of like the quality of certain foods... I feel like. So that's where I'm, like, willing to even take, like, 1 or 2 pounds off and switch to the other kind.

spk_1: I actually was a writer once. Yeah, I did it... like, this is actually so funny. I did it during COVID with my mom. So I guess I did kind of know someone that was a writer. But we did it for like two days or something... for... oh, what? I can't even remember which company it was for. I think it was for Uber Eats. But we, like, did that for a whole two days. Yeah, just me and my mom. Like, it was kind of a fun experience. Yeah!

spk_1: Like, some concerns that I would have... Is it, like I said, it was quite time-consuming for what I was doing. I can't remember how much money we made, um, but I think for certain deliveries, it can be quite... like, sometimes the money isn't always worth it for, like, how far you're driving. And on top of that, you have to pay for gas as well, which, where we were living at the time, gas was already quite expensive. So driving that far... So I guess, like, considering the profit that you're making from, like, the amount that you're spending on the actual, like, travel and the money that you're earning from the delivery is, I feel like, not so fulfilling. And that would kind of hold me back from being a delivery driver.

So what we've done more often recently is doing food orders together. It kind of saves some time from going into town and doing a food shop that way. So we'll... if it's from, like, Sainsbury's or Tesco's, we'll all have, like, one computer, and then one person will do the big order, and then we'll separate the cost. Usually, we'll do that with, like, three people max per order.
